geometry.rationale: Figure 1 constructs its dashed lines using volumetric cylinders rather than 1px flat WebGL lines, and correctly introduces a non-planar height-warping parameter to mathematically demonstrate the defining geometric characteristic of a bilinear patch.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides a more comprehensive set of interactions, including an additional parameter slider for corner height scale and, most notably, a fully animated step-by-step tutorial triggered by clicking elements, whereas Figure 2 only shows static text popups on click.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly renders the base of the linear interpolation diagram as a 1D line, whereas Figure 1 incorrectly draws it as a 2D plane, and Figure 2 avoids the severe label overlaps present in Figure 1.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 includes all required labels within the visible area, whereas Figure 2 cuts off multiple important labels on the left and right edges of the canvas, despite Figure 1 having some overlap between axis labels and captions.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 introduces a corner height scale control that interactively demonstrates the doubly-ruled nature of the bilinear patch (straight lines on a curved surface), which significantly deepens the pedagogical value compared to Figure 2.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a more engaging pedagogical experience than Figure 2 by featuring an animated step-by-step tutorial and interactive height warping, whereas Figure 2 is limited to static text popups. Additionally, Figure 1 uses clear volumetric cylinders for dashed lines and keeps all labels visible within the canvas, while Figure 2 relies on flat 1px lines and cuts off multiple important labels along its edges. Although Figure 2 renders its 1D base slightly more faithfully, Figure 1's superior interactivity and geometric representation make it the overall better visualization.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 uses genuine 3D primitives (spheres, boxes, cones) for the feature markers and curved 3D paths for their trajectories, whereas Figure 2 improperly uses flat 2D plane geometries for the shapes and straight polylines.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers more pedagogically meaningful interactions, such as clicking a concept to isolate its specific path through both towers and a slider to dynamically adjust layer spacing, whereas Figure 1 only offers basic global toggles for paths and cues.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 correctly maintains the left-to-right layout of the image and text encoders with reasonable proportions, whereas Figure 2 reverses the columns, grossly distorts the scale of the top circle, and suffers from severe text overlapping.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 correctly positions the Image and Text encoder labels on the left and right respectively and includes the top title, whereas Figure 2 swaps the encoder labels, omits the title, and has severe text overlap.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 allows the user to click a concept to isolate and highlight its paired trajectory through both encoders and into the joint space, making the concept of cross-modal alignment much clearer than Figure 1's visual clutter of all paths shown simultaneously.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is selected as the winner because it correctly maintains the left-to-right encoder layout, uses genuine 3D primitives, and features well-positioned labels without overlapping text. Although Figure 2 excels in interactivity and conceptual clarity by allowing users to isolate specific concept trajectories, its severe structural flaws—such as reversed columns, distorted scale, flat 2D geometries, and cluttered text—make it less effective overall.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reproduces the textbook's 3D geometry by including the characteristic wireframe Gaussian envelopes bounding the top and bottom of the Gabor ripples, and uses a clever diagonal layout to maintain distinct, non-overlapping isometric views for each panel.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers far more comprehensive developer-built interactions, featuring three separate parameter controls (component type, orientation, and frequency) and an animated step-through tutorial triggered by clicking the 3D panels, whereas Figure 1 only provides a single frequency slider and basic selection highlighting.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 successfully reproduces the three distinct subplots with the correct color gradients and shapes, whereas Figure 2 presents a confusing, zoomed-in view with overlapping elements and an incorrect wireframe overlay.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 correctly places the (a), (b), and (c) labels below their respective plots matching the original, while Figure 2's labels are cut off, misaligned, and appear out of order.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 allows the user to manipulate the orientation of the 2D Gabor filter—a fundamental property of these filters—and adds a wireframe Gaussian envelope overlay that perfectly visually explains the mathematical multiplication of the carrier wave by the spatial window, whereas Figure 1 lacks orientation controls and only displays the raw surfaces.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 is the overall winner because it offers vastly superior interactivity, including three parameter controls and an animated tutorial, and provides better conceptual depth by incorporating wireframe Gaussian envelopes to explain the mathematical structure, whereas Figure 1 only features a basic frequency slider. However, Figure 1 is more faithful to the original layout with clear, correctly placed labels, whereas Figure 2 suffers from cut-off labels and a somewhat confusing zoomed-in presentation.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately constructs orthogonal drop lines to the coordinate planes and dynamically projects the 3D point onto the image plane using correct pinhole geometry, whereas Figure 1 uses arbitrary, geometrically incorrect endpoints for its guide lines (e.g., dropping to Y=0.42 and Z=P.z*0.8) and fails to update the 2D projection during interaction.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 provides far more pedagogically useful interactivity by dynamically updating the entire projection geometry (the 3D point, its 2D projection, the ray, and coordinate lines) when using the sliders, and it includes guided step-through animations, whereas Figure 1 only rotates a disconnected 'ghost' point without updating the projection mechanics.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 avoids the severe text overlap seen on the Y-axis in Figure 1 and correctly labels the plane's local axes 'x' and 'y' instead of 'x'' and 'y''.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly includes and spaces the 'x' and 'y' labels on the projection plane, whereas Figure 1 uses poorly legible 'x'' and 'y'' and suffers from overlapping text for the 'Yaw'/'Pitch' and 'theta_X'/'theta_Y' labels.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 accurately demonstrates the pedagogical concept by dynamically updating the entire projection geometry (the 3D point, the projection ray, and the 2D image point) when the Euler angles are changed, whereas Figure 1 only rotates a disconnected ghost point without showing its effect on the image plane.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 accurately demonstrates the projection concept by dynamically updating the coordinate drop lines, projection ray, and 2D image point during interaction, whereas Figure 1 fails to update projection mechanics and relies on geometrically incorrect guide lines. Furthermore, Figure 2 provides clearly spaced labels, avoiding the severe text overlap and poorly formatted axis labels present in Figure 1.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 accurately utilizes the standard Z-axis for depth in its camera coordinate projection and dynamically parameterizes the geometric relationships, whereas Figure 2 unconventionally projects along the X-axis and relies on a hardcoded plane patch.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers rich, pedagogically useful interactions including sliders to dynamically alter motion direction and point depth, as well as animated narrative steps, whereas Figure 2 only provides a simple visibility toggle for the motion vectors.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 includes all the necessary text labels (such as O and P, which are missing in Figure 1) and correctly matches the angle and direction of the red vectors from the original, whereas Figure 1 points them horizontally.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly includes all labels from the original (O, p, p-dot, P, P-dot), whereas Figure 1 is missing the 'O' and 'P' labels entirely.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 includes interactive sliders that allow students to explore how depth and 3D motion direction directly affect the projected 2D velocity, providing a significantly better pedagogical demonstration of the core concepts.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a significantly stronger pedagogical experience through interactive sliders that demonstrate how depth and 3D motion direction affect projected velocity, whereas Figure 2 offers only a basic visibility toggle. Additionally, Figure 1 correctly uses standard projection geometry with dynamic parametrization, whereas Figure 2 relies on unconventional projections and hardcoded elements, even though it is more faithful to the original image's static labels.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly implements the specific mathematical formulas for the continuous and discrete Laplacian spectra (band-pass shapes and discrete anisotropy), whereas Figure 2 hallucinates arbitrary surface equations that do not represent Laplacian filters.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 includes a functional UI button to toggle a visual ring and changes scene visuals (scaling/opacity) when hovering and clicking panels, whereas Figure 2 lacks UI controls and relies solely on external postMessage events for click interactions without updating the 3D scene.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 includes all four surface plots from the reference image, whereas Figure 2 completely omits two of the required plots, making Figure 1 much more faithful despite its incorrect diagonal layout and slightly mismatched red highlights.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 accurately replicates the correct label text ('u', 'v') and italicized style of the original, whereas Figure 1 inappropriately concatenates axis and subfigure labels (e.g., 'v.a)') and uses an awkward, less readable font.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 accurately computes and displays the true mathematical functions for the Laplacian of Gaussian and the discrete five-point Laplacian spectra, whereas Figure 2 hallucinates arbitrary, incorrect mathematical formulas with random noise.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is much stronger because it faithfully implements the required mathematical formulas for the Laplacian spectra and includes all four requested surface plots alongside functional interactive UI features. In contrast, Figure 2 completely misses the mark by hallucinating arbitrary equations, omitting two of the plots entirely, and failing to provide functional in-scene UI controls, even though it handles axis labels slightly better.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 perfectly reproduces the standard MATLAB-style 3D stem plot geometry for a 2D binomial filter with correct bounding box walls, tick placements, and accurately sized stems and caps; Figure 2 includes extraneous geometry (a frequency response surface) which deviates from the core textbook illustration of the spatial filter.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers highly granular, data-driven interactions, allowing users to hover and click individual stems to see their exact values and outer-product calculations, whereas Figure 2 only offers group-level tooltips and a simple visibility toggle dropdown.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 correctly renders a single point per stem to match the original data, whereas Figure 2 incorrectly places multiple points along individual stems.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly places the 'm' and 'n' axis labels along the outer bottom edges and maintains good readability, whereas Figure 1 features cluttered, squished text and misplaces the 'm' label floating randomly inside the grid.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 correctly maintains spatial domain concepts, whereas Figure 2 commits a severe conceptual error by plotting the continuous frequency response on the exact same axes as the discrete spatial variables n and m.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a conceptually accurate spatial domain representation and faithfully reproduces the 3D stem plot geometry with individual interactive points, whereas Figure 2 commits a severe conceptual error by overlaying a continuous frequency response on discrete spatial axes and incorrectly places multiple points along individual stems. Although Figure 2 features better-placed and more readable axis labels, the fundamental conceptual and geometric accuracy of Figure 1 makes it the vastly superior visualization.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 uses Cylinder geometries for edges and curves to overcome WebGL's 1px line width limitation, resulting in proper proportions and visibility compared to Figure 2's hair-thin LineBasicMaterial primitives.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers a significantly richer set of meaningful interactions, including a layer-focus slider, flow direction dropdown, and interactive elements that trigger step-by-step narrations with animated particles, whereas Figure 2 only provides basic highlighting buttons.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 successfully includes the critical side text labels and attempts to angle the internal dashed lines, whereas Figure 1 entirely omits the side text and uses strictly vertical lines, despite Figure 2's overlapping text and wireframe plane artifacts.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 includes all required text labels despite poor placement and overlap, whereas Figure 1 completely omits the crucial side labels 'Representation learning' and 'Generative modeling'.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 correctly uses animated particles that reverse direction to demonstrate the top-down flow of generative modeling, whereas Figure 2 shows mapping curves that statically point upward even when highlighting the generative mode, which contradicts the downward side arrow and misrepresents the concept.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 offers superior geometry by using visible cylinders instead of Figure 2's hair-thin lines, and it accurately demonstrates the top-down conceptual flow using animated, reversing particles. Although Figure 2 successfully includes the side text labels that Figure 1 completely omits, its static upward-pointing curves misrepresent the generative mode, and its labels suffer from severe overlap.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly renders the complete 3D box filter kernel, whereas Figure 2 contains a fatal JavaScript error (calling a missing updateLabels function) that breaks its execution and prevents the geometry from being built.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 2 offers far more meaningful interactions, including sliders to dynamically adjust the filter dimensions (N and M) and a step-through tutorial sequence, whereas Figure 1 only allows toggling normalization for a fixed 3x3 filter.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 successfully renders the data points, stems, and grid planes present in the original, whereas Figure 2 completely fails to display any data and only shows a faint bounding box.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 clearly displays all required axis and tick labels with reasonable readability, whereas Figure 2's labels are microscopic and completely illegible.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 provides a fully functional and conceptually accurate representation of the box filter kernel and normalization, whereas Figure 2 contains a missing function call that completely breaks the visualization.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 successfully and faithfully renders the complete 3D box filter kernel, data points, and legible labels, offering a fully functional visualization. In contrast, while Figure 2 attempts to provide richer interactivity with sliders and a tutorial sequence, it suffers from a fatal JavaScript error that completely breaks its geometry rendering, failing to display the data and featuring microscopic, illegible labels.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly models the image planes based on the cameras' optical axes looking towards the scene, correctly placing the epipoles outside the drawn image rectangles and clipping the epipolar lines to the visible planes. Figure 2 inaccurately centers its physical image plane rectangles exactly on the epipoles, which implies the cameras are looking directly at each other rather than at the scene.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides more meaningful controls, including two independent sliders for 3D point positioning (X and depth) and a click-to-advance step-through animation, whereas Figure 2 limits movement to a single 1D slider path with no narrative steps.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly positions the epipoles on the image planes and draws accurate epipolar lines connecting them to the projected points, whereas Figure 1 leaves the epipoles floating in the middle and draws incorrect intersecting lines on the planes.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 accurately rotates the 'Epipolar line' labels and correctly places the 'e1' and 'e2' labels at the plane intersections, avoiding the severe overlapping text and floating labels seen in Figure 1.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 correctly anchors the image planes so that the epipoles (the intersection of the baseline with the image planes) lie within the drawn rectangular boundaries of the planes, perfectly matching standard textbook diagrams. Figure 1 calculates the epipoles correctly but places them significantly outside the drawn image plane boundaries, causing them to float disconnected in space which conceptually confuses the relationship between the epipole and the image plane.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 offers a clearer conceptual representation by ensuring the epipoles remain grounded within the image plane boundaries and properly formatting its text labels for readability. Conversely, although Figure 1 provides more versatile interactive controls, it suffers from visually confusing floating epipoles outside the planes and severe text overlapping that degrades the overall instructional quality.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 constructs actual 3D planes and uses true ray-plane intersection to compute the projected point p', whereas Figure 2 completely fakes the geometry by placing all elements at Z=0 and hardcoding an artificial 2D translation formula to move the point.

interactivity.rationale: Both figures include a depth slider and raycast-based educational tooltips, but Figure 2 adds the ability to directly drag the 3D point inside the canvas to adjust its depth, providing a more intuitive and pedagogically effective direct-manipulation interaction.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 includes the loop arrow at point P which is missing in Figure 1, and its curved transformation arrows more accurately match the shape and placement of those in the original figure.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 successfully includes the important self-loop annotation at P and correctly renders the long curved arrow to p', whereas Figure 1 completely misses the self-loop and features a distorted, broken arrow, though Figure 2 does suffer from point labels overlapping their respective red dots.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 accurately computes the reprojection of the 3D point onto the reference camera plane using true geometric intersection, whereas Figure 2 fakes the projection path using arbitrary hardcoded translations that do not correctly model the mathematics.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 excels in mathematical accuracy by using true 3D planes and ray-plane intersections to compute the reprojection, whereas Figure 2 completely fakes the geometry with arbitrary 2D translations. Conversely, Figure 2 provides a more intuitive direct-manipulation interactivity and features far more faithful annotations, such as correct curved arrows and a crucial self-loop that Figure 1 completely misses, resulting in a genuine split between structural accuracy and visual/interactive presentation.
